title: var, let, const
description: Learn the three ways to declare variables in JavaScript and when to use each.

blocks.content:
When you write a program, you need to hold onto information — a username, a score, an app setting. In JavaScript, you store a value in a variable and give it a name you can use later.

Every variable starts with a keyword. Use const for values that will never change, let for values that will, and var for legacy code — though modern JavaScript prefers let and const.

question.question: You're building a fitness app that tracks how many steps the user has taken today. The count starts at zero and increases as they walk. Which keyword should you use?
question.explanation: A step counter needs to increase throughout the day, which means the variable must be reassignable. let is the only keyword here that allows reassignment to a new value.

blocks.content:
const stands for constant — a value you lock in place. Once you create a const variable, you cannot reassign it — that means you cannot update it to hold a new value. JavaScript will throw an error if you try.

Use const as your default. Only reach for let when the value will need to change — like a score that ticks up, or a form field the user can edit.

question.question: A const variable cannot be ___ after it is created.
question.explanation: Reassignment means pointing the variable name at a new value entirely. Note: const prevents reassignment, but if the value is an object or array, you can still modify its contents — just not replace the reference itself.

blocks.content:
var is the original JavaScript keyword, but it has a quirk: it ignores block boundaries. A var declared inside an if statement is visible outside it — which can cause subtle bugs that are hard to trace.

Modern JavaScript uses let and const instead. You will encounter var in older code, but there is no reason to use it in new code.

question.question: Match each keyword to its key characteristic.
Order these keywords from most to least restrictive.
question.explanation: const is most restrictive — it blocks reassignment entirely. let allows reassignment but respects block scope. var is least restrictive — it allows reassignment and ignores block boundaries.
